SF Business_Objectives__c
Demo call 23-Jun-2026 (Avoma 254c947c): Martina Winkler (digitalization lead), Iulia Georgescu (Global Category Manager - Direct), Daniel Quaschnok (indirect material & services), David Balke (buyer)
Avoma notes: 'reduce manual effort and triage volume', 'improve visibility and cycle times', 'fragmented risk data across Excel files', '~1,500 suppliers mentioned for data consolidation'
metrics.narrative: The business problem is clearly articulated: Kromberg & Schubert is modernizing its procurement function with an end-to-end S2P platform to improve process standardization, user experience, supplier risk management, and automation as part of an EY-led digital transformation (Customer_Business_Problem__c, demo call 23-Jun-2026). Martina Winkler (digitalization project lead) stated the evaluation is for a configurable, AI-driven platform covering intake, sourcing, supplier onboarding, contracts, risk, reporting, and ERP integration. Key pain points were named: fragmented risk data across Excel files and systems, manual presourcing committee approval snapshots, ad-hoc demand submission channels, and the need to consolidate ~1,500 suppliers with their risk scorecards (demo call 23-Jun, speakers Iulia Georgescu, Daniel Quaschnok, David Balke). However, NO quantified impact has been stated — no cost savings target, cycle-time reduction, headcount freed, or ROI model. The buyer described the need as 'reduce manual effort and triage volume' and 'improve visibility and cycle times' but did not quantify the baseline or the goal. Iulia Georgescu asked about savings calculation flexibility during the demo, confirming savings are a priority, but no dollar or percentage figure was anchored. The business objectives are qualitative: 'better process, better UX, better risk visibility' — not tied to a number. For a €200k deal, the absence of a quantified value case is a gap. The next step is to co-author an ROI model anchoring savings, cycle-time improvement, or compliance risk reduction before commercials.

champion.narrative: Champion is undeveloped. Martina Winkler (Head of Advanced Supplier Development) holds the Decision Maker contact role and organized the 23-Jun demo call (organizer email martina.winkler@ksab.kroschu.com), signaling ownership of the digitalization project. She opened the demo call by framing the objective as 'evaluating an end-to-end S2P platform' and confirmed EY is leading the digital transformation program. However, there is NO evidence of advocacy — no introductions to the Economic Buyer, no pushing back on competitors (no competitor is even named), no driving the agenda forward post-demo. In fact, the opposite: Martina requested 4 deliverables on the 23-Jun call (recording, presentation, Coface details, workshop scheduling) and the Kromberg team submitted the RFI on 25-Jun, but 6 days have passed with no follow-up from either side. If Martina were an active champion, she would have chased Zycus for the deliverables or confirmed next steps. Silence suggests she is a project coordinator, not an advocate. Daniel Quaschnok (Global Category Manager, Evaluator role) was highly engaged on the call (asked 10+ questions about presourcing automation, configurability, risk integration, contract tracking) and may be a stronger champion candidate, but no advocacy evidence exists yet. Alexander Friedrichs (responsible for digital transformation, per attendee intro) was on the call but contributed minimally. No other stakeholder showed advocacy behavior. Current champion strength: weak to developing. The solution-fit workshop is the next venue to assess and convert champion potential — Zycus must identify who internally is pushing for Zycus vs other platforms, and what they need to win the internal argument.

competition.narrative: Competitive landscape is genuinely unknown — a major blind spot. No competitor is named in any Salesforce field (Competitors__c null, Others_Competitors_Please_specify__c null, Existing_vendor__c null, Replacing_What__c null, Closed_Lost_Reason_Code__c null). No competitor was mentioned in the 23-Jun demo call transcript (no speaker named SAP Ariba, Coupa, Ivalua, Jaggaer, GEP, or any other S2P platform). No Next_Step__c or Next_Step_History__c entry mentions a competitive shortlist, parallel demo, or benchmark. The buyer is running an EY-led digital transformation program and submitted an RFI, which typically implies a multi-vendor evaluation, but Zycus has not surfaced who else is in the running. The demo call was titled 'Foundation | Demo Session' and Peter Scharbert (EY partner) framed it as a 'system demo of Zycus iConsole for the S2C use case' — language that implies Zycus is one option being evaluated, not the only one. However, no explicit competitive discussion occurred. This is a knowledge gap, not a hygiene gap. The risk: if Kromberg is benchmarking Zycus against SAP Ariba (strong in automotive), Coupa (market leader), or Ivalua (configurability peer) and Zycus has not built a competitive wedge (Merlin AI, Teams embedding, ease of use, automotive supplier risk), the deal risks being outflanked by a competitor with better incumbent positioning or champion alignment. The next move is to surface the competitive field during the solution-fit workshop — ask directly: 'Who else are you evaluating, and what are the key differentiators you are testing across vendors?' Then build the wedge around Zycus strengths (Merlin agentic AI, Teams-native intake, configurability, automotive Tier-1 references).

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is confirmed and articulated by multiple buyer stakeholders on the 23-Jun demo call, with clear pain owners. (1) Fragmented risk and supplier data: David Balke and Iulia Georgescu described multiple fragmented risk/score sources across Excel files and other systems, requiring manual consolidation and upload of ~1,500 suppliers with scorecards (pain point note, demo call 23-Jun). Pain owner: category managers and buyers managing supplier risk. (2) Manual presourcing committee approval process: Daniel Quaschnok and Martina Winkler described the current workflow where presourcing committee reviews demand via manual report snapshots for approvals, creating triage bottlenecks (current workflow note, demo call 23-Jun). Pain owner: presourcing committee and category teams. (3) Ad-hoc demand intake: requesters currently submit demands via ad-hoc channels and Excel files, with no policy enforcement or triage, creating unnecessary sourcing work (pain point note, demo call 23-Jun, Martina Winkler and Asmita Mukherjee discussion). Pain owner: requesters and category buyers. (4) Direct-material RFQ complexity: Iulia Georgescu (Global Category Manager - Direct) stated that hundreds/thousands of parts require detailed, customizable templates (quantities by year, part numbers, releases, locations, projects) for RFQs, and current tools lack that flexibility (pain point note, demo call 23-Jun). Pain owner: Iulia Georgescu and direct-material buyers. (5) Lack of contract visibility and obligation tracking: Daniel Quaschnok asked about contract cycle-time dashboards and tracking limited-term contracts (2-3 years), confirming current lack of visibility (pain point and question notes, demo call 23-Jun). Pain owner: contract and category managers. All five pain points are confirmed, named, and tied to specific stakeholders. The pain is real and operational, not inferred.

economic_buyer.narrative: Economic Buyer is unmapped and unengaged. For a €200k S2P platform deal at Kromberg & Schubert (a global automotive Tier-1 with ~€3.5bn revenue), budget authority almost certainly resides at CFO or VP Finance level, likely Carsten Meyer (CFO, account contact) or Andreas Kopp (General Manager Purchasing, account contact). Neither was on the 23-Jun demo call, and neither holds a contact role on this opportunity. No Salesforce field identifies the EB (X10a_Sponsor__c null, Who_will_approve_budget__c null, Executive_Sponsor_Identified__c false). The demo call was attended by operational and category management stakeholders (Martina Winkler as digitalization lead is the Decision Maker per contact role, but she is a program manager, not a budget holder). EY's Peter Scharbert (partner) was on the call, but his role is advisory (leading the digital transformation program), not commercial. No conversation about budget, approval chain, or CFO/executive sponsorship appeared in the transcript. The absence of EB engagement before commercials is a high risk — when pricing surfaces, the deal may stall at an approval gate Zycus has not mapped or prepped. The next move is to ask Martina Winkler or the EY team who holds budget authority for this program and to secure an intro to that person within the next 30 days, ideally before the solution-fit workshop.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are emerging but not yet formalized. The buyer articulated 10+ evaluation dimensions during the 23-Jun demo call, captured in questions and feature requests: (1) Microsoft Teams integration and conversational intake (Merlin Intake praised by multiple attendees); (2) configurability — forms, workflows, approvals, pricing templates must be customizable by the buyer's admin team without Zycus support (asked by the relevant stakeholder, Iulia Georgescu, confirmed by Asmita Mukherjee); (3) presourcing committee workflow automation and policy enforcement to avoid unnecessary triage (Daniel Quaschnok asked about automatic routing for MRO articles); (4) Coface and other risk-provider integrations (explicitly requested by the relevant stakeholder, integration timeline estimated 2-3 weeks if Coface provides API access); (5) bulk Excel upload and mapping for ~1,500 suppliers and risk scorecards (David Balke, Iulia Georgescu); (6) direct-material RFQ complexity — hundreds/thousands of parts with customizable templates by year, location, project (Iulia Georgescu); (7) contract intelligence — InstaReview risk highlighting, obligation extraction, cycle-time tracking (Daniel Quaschnok asked about contract cycle-time dashboards); (8) AI/agentic roadmap — category management agent, autonomous negotiation, onboarding agent (roadmap items mentioned, buyers expect 'within months'); (9) end-to-end integrated S2P with sourcing-to-contract linkage; (10) ease of use and 'no UI' for requesters. These are confirmed as must-haves from the call. However, NO formal scoring rubric, BRD, or RFP decision matrix has been shared — the RFI received 25-Jun-2026 may contain it, but Zycus has not logged having read it. The solution-fit workshop mentioned in Next_Step__c is the planned venue to formalize and validate these criteria. Until that workshop happens and the BRD is reviewed, criteria remain partial.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AI fit signal is present but not quantified. The buyer's Business_Objectives__c states 'Requires AI-driven procurement, supplier onboarding, contract lifecycle management, reporting, and supplier risk management.' The Description mentions 'Agentic AI automation' as a key focus area. Martina Winkler framed the evaluation as seeking an 'AI-powered, configurable end-to-end S2P platform' on the demo call. Asmita Mukherjee positioned Merlin Intake (Teams-embedded conversational AI) and the AI agent roadmap (category management, autonomous negotiation, onboarding) as core differentiators, and buyers responded positively. However, AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, AIS_Why__c are all null (no AI excitement score on record). No explicit buyer statement quantifying AI appetite (e.g. 'we need AI to replace manual category analysis', 'AI is a top-3 selection criterion') appeared in the transcript. The buyer's interest in AI is confirmed but not rated. Based on the evidence, the buyer is AI Curious: they want AI-driven procurement and praised Merlin Intake, but AI is not yet the primary differentiation axis (because the competitive field is unknown and no AI wedge has been built). The next move is to quantify AI fit during the solution-fit workshop: ask 'How important is AI agent capability (category management, autonomous negotiation, onboarding) in your vendor selection, and what AI capabilities are the other platforms offering?'

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning was a core Zycus differentiator on the 23-Jun demo call. Asmita Mukherjee led with Merlin agentic platform (AI) on top of a single-source data core, positioning Zycus as 'Merlin agentic platform (AI) on top of a single-source data core; Teams-integrated Merlin Intake aims for no UI by guiding most user tasks within Teams.' However, two risks: (1) AI agents are roadmap, not GA today — if buyer expects immediate deployment, this is a gap; (2) no explicit buyer statement of AI appetite or AI differentiation vs competitors (no competitor is named, so no AI wedge was built). customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong product-market fit demonstrated on 23-Jun demo call. Buyers responded positively to Merlin Intake (Teams-embedded conversational intake praised by multiple attendees), platform configurability (forms, workflows, approvals, pricing templates customizable by buyer's admin team), presourcing committee automation, risk integrations (Coface, Ecovadis, D&B), bulk Excel upload/mapping, direct-material RFQ flexibility, contract intelligence (InstaReview, obligation extraction, cycle-time tracking), AI/agentic roadmap (category management agent, autonomous negotiation, onboarding agent), and end-to-end integrated S2P with sourcing-to-contract linkage. Attendees confirmed Zycus appeared to align with their needs and agreed to digest the demo and follow up in the project. Key fit areas: (1) Microsoft Teams integration — Merlin Intake praised as 'no UI' front door for requesters; (2) configurability — buyers confirmed ability to customize forms, workflows, approvals, templates themselves post-implementation; (3) presourcing automation — Daniel Quaschnok's question about automatic routing for MRO articles confirmed as supported; (4) Coface integration — requested by David Balke, estimated 2-3 weeks if Coface provides API access; (5) bulk data upload — confirmed for ~1,500 suppliers and risk scorecards; (6) direct-material complexity — Iulia Georgescu's RFQ template needs (quantities by year, location, project) confirmed as customizable; (7) contract cycle-time visibility — Daniel Quaschnok's dashboard question confirmed as supported. Two gaps surfaced: (1) Coface integration is not out-of-the-box, requires 2-3 weeks custom work if Coface cooperates (risk if Coface integration is a must-have gate); (2) some AI agent capabilities (category management, onboarding) are roadmap items 'within months', not available today — if buyer expects immediate AI agent deployment, this is a gap. Overall fit is strong, with minor execution risk on Coface and AI agent timing.

deltas.subject: Develop a champion and multi-thread beyond Martina Winkler. deltas.subject: Co-author a quantified ROI model before commercials. The buyer confirmed pain (fragmented risk data, manual presourcing, ad-hoc intake, direct-material RFQ complexity, lack of contract visibility) but no number is anchored — no cost savings target, cycle-time reduction, or headcount freed. Iulia Georgescu asked about savings calculation flexibility on the demo call, confirming ROI is a priority. Build the model with the buyer's finance or procurement analytics team: quantify baseline manual effort hours, current supplier risk exposure, presourcing bottleneck cost, and project the savings/ROI Zycus will deliver. Without a quantified value case, pricing approval will be harder.

deltas.subject: Develop Martina Winkler (Decision Maker) as champion through value co-creation and internal selling support. deltas.subject: Map and engage the Economic Buyer (likely CFO Carsten Meyer) before moving to commercials. value.flag: Surface and neutralize the competitive field during the solution-fit workshop. With EY-supported vendor selection and a formal RFI process, Kromberg is almost certainly evaluating 3-5 vendors (SAP Ariba, Ivalua, Coupa, Jaggaer likely). Without knowing who Zycus is up against, the deal team cannot position the Merlin agentic AI wedge (embedded in Teams, conversational intake, no-code configurability) against competitor gaps. Ask directly during the workshop: 'Who else are you evaluating? What is your current procurement system? What are the shortcomings driving this evaluation?' Build a differentiation narrative anchored to Kromberg's stated priorities (self-service, automation, flexibility) within the next 14 days.

business_case.evidence: Business problem confirmed: modernize procurement with end-to-end S2P platform to improve process standardization, user experience, supplier risk management, and automation as part of EY-led digital transformation (Customer_Business_Problem__c, demo call 23-Jun). Pain confirmed: fragmented risk data across Excel files, manual presourcing committee approval snapshots, ad-hoc demand intake, direct-material RFQ complexity, lack of contract visibility (demo call 23-Jun, speakers Iulia Georgescu, Daniel Quaschnok, David Balke). Qualitative impact articulated: 'reduce manual effort and triage volume', 'improve visibility and cycle times'. However, no quantified ROI — no cost savings target, cycle-time reduction percentage, headcount freed, or compliance risk reduction number. Iulia Georgescu asked about savings calculation flexibility on demo, confirming savings are priority, but no dollar or percentage figure anchored. Business case is qualitative, not quantified.
